title: The Event Loop
summary: JavaScript does one thing at a time.
The event loop lets it stay responsive while waiting.
Slow tasks are set aside, then handled when ready.
This is why the page does not freeze.

sections.text: JavaScript can only do one thing at a time, yet pages stay smooth while loading data in the background. The trick behind this is a quiet manager called the event loop.

sections.title: One Thing at a Time
sections.text: JavaScript runs on a single track, handling one piece of code at a time. If it stopped to wait for every slow task, the whole page would freeze. So instead of waiting, it sets slow tasks aside and keeps going.

sections.title: How the Loop Works
sections.text: When a slow task like a timer or a data fetch finishes, its follow up code waits in a line. The event loop is the manager that checks, whenever the main track is free, and runs the next waiting item. This is how waiting work gets done without ever blocking the page.

media.alt: A main track with finished tasks waiting in a line to be run

sections.title: A Chef with a Timer
sections.text: A good chef does not stare at the oven. They put a dish in, set a timer, and start chopping vegetables. When the timer dings, they handle the dish at the next free moment. The event loop is that timer and the habit of checking it, so nothing sits idle and nothing blocks.

media.alt: A chef setting a timer and chopping, then handling the dish on the ding

sections.title: How the Event Loop Helps
items: Code runs one piece at a time on the main track.
A slow task gets set aside to finish on its own.
The main track keeps running other code.
When the slow task is done, its follow up waits in line.
The event loop runs it as soon as the track is free.

sections.text: This is why a long, heavy calculation can freeze a page, but waiting on a server does not. The calculation hogs the single track, while the server wait is set aside politely.

terms.definition: The manager that runs waiting tasks when the track is free.
JavaScript handling one piece of code at a time.
Work set aside to finish on its own.
The line of finished tasks waiting to run.

questions.text: How many things does JavaScript do at a time?
questions.explanation: JavaScript runs on a single track, doing one thing at a time.

questions.text: What does the event loop do?
options: Runs waiting tasks when the main track is free
questions.explanation: The event loop runs the next waiting task whenever the main track is free.

questions.text: Why does a heavy calculation freeze the page?
questions.explanation: A heavy calculation hogs the single track, so nothing else can run until it finishes.
